p: Analyze your problem's inputs, data structures, and constraints to isolate the optimal algorithmic execution pattern.
Primary Implementation Engine: Java (OpenJDK 21+)
Interactive Prototyping: Jupyter Lab with Java Kernel / IntelliJ Scratchpads
Target Metric: Optimize $O(n^2) $ naive approaches down to $O(n) $ or $O(\log n) $ performance.

The Sliding Window pattern converts nested loops into a single linear pass by maintaining a continuous sub-segment ("window") over an array or string that expands or contracts dynamically based on problem criteria.
Input State: Problem mentions contiguous subarrays, sub-segments, or substrings.
Objective: Finding the maximum, minimum, or optimal longest/shortest window length matching a specific target condition.

l: 💻 Java Implementation: Minimum Size Subarray Sum
p: Establishes the left boundary pointer of our tracking window to manage contraction phases.
Dynamically expands the window by absorbing the current element at the right boundary pointer into our running summation tracker.
Triggers a contraction check loop the moment our current window sum matches or exceeds the required target constraint threshold.
Records and isolates the smallest window length found so far by evaluating the current distance between our right and left index points.
Systematically subtracts the element trailing at the left index boundary to prepare the window sum for a size contraction.
Increments the left boundary forward to physically shrink the window size while searching for a more optimal, smaller sub-segment.
The Two-Pointer technique optimizes search boundaries by managing two independent index references that move toward each other or in tandem, bypassing the need for nested O(n^2) loops.
Input State: The linear data structure (array or arraylist) is sorted or organized sequentially.
Objective: Locating a pair, triplet, or subarray that satisfies a strict target evaluation.

l: 💻 Java Implementation: Two Sum II (Sorted Input)
p: Initialize the left pointer reference at the absolute beginning of the sorted collection (index 0).
Initialize the right pointer reference at the final terminal index position of the array dataset.
The problem constraints dictate a 1-indexed return array output, so we offset our 0-indexed positions by adding 1.
If our combined value falls short of the target value, we increment left to pull a larger integer layer into the computation.
If our combined value exceeds the target value, we decrement right to bring a smaller value scale into the calculation.
This page displays all five card layout variations available in the Retype framework.
Best for a single, prominent focal link with an image displayed beside the text.
Best for stacking items. Consecutive vertical cards automatically snap into a responsive grid column layout.
Best for a space-saving navigation block. It strips out heavy padding, footers, and margins to keep elements inline.
Best for index rows or quick dashboards. It displays a narrow, clean layout that guarantees icon visibility.
Best for app-pickers or grid navigation. It generates a tile-style button that emphasizes the icon and title while ignoring body text entirely.
